column3: 6 months ago, it took us 40 hours every quarter to clean HubSpot .
column12:
6 months ago, it took us 40 hours every quarter to clean HubSpot .
Today, 32 Claude Code skills do it in 5 minutes. I'm sharing the full GitHub repo for free.

Here's what's inside:

Audit & Planning (2 skills)
↳ hubspot-audit: scans your CRM and scores every issue
↳ implementation-roadmap: turns the audit into a clear fix plan

Database Hygiene (6 skills)
↳ delete-no-email-contacts: removes contacts with no email
↳ suppress-hard-bouncers: blocks bounced contacts so you don't burn your domain.
↳ dedupe-companies: finds and merges duplicate company records.
↳ reassign-orphan-records: moves contacts and deals from inactive owners to active reps.
↳ suppress-ghost-contacts: suppress unresponsive contacts
↳ suppress-unsubscribers: keeps you compliant and stops you wasting marketing spend.

Data Enrichment (5 skills)
↳ populate-company-names: fills in missing company names from existing data.
↳ populate-industries: adds the right industry to every contact.
↳ normalize-geo-values: cleans up country and state fields so they all match.
↳ assign-unowned-contacts: routes unowned contacts to the right rep automatically.
↳ repair-lifecycle-stages: fixes contacts stuck in the wrong sales stage.

Segmentation & Scoring (3 skills)
↳ define-icp-tiers: builds your ICP tiers based on company size, industry, and fit.
↳ configure-lead-scoring: sets up a real lead score based on behavior and engagement.
↳ setup-smart-lists: creates the lists you need for ICP, lifecycle, and engagement.

Automation Workflows (4 skills)
↳ new-contact-onboarding-workflow: cleans up every new contact
↳ engagement-suppression-workflow: removes contacts who haven't engaged in months.
↳ lifecycle-stage-workflow: moves contacts to the next stage when they actually do something.
↳ bounce-threshold-workflow: suppresses contacts as soon as they bounce too many times.

Ongoing Maintenance (12 skills)
↳ weekly-portal-checkup: 5 minutes a week to keep things clean.
↳ quarterly-portal-review: A full sweep every 3 months.
↳ cleanup-lists, cleanup-forms, cleanup-workflows, cleanup-dashboards.
↳ cleanup-properties, cleanup-lead-owners, cleanup-deal-pipelines.
↳ review-bounce-contacts, populate-geo-data, setup-segment-lists.

Every skill follows the same simple flow:
Plan → Before → Execute → After

You get the plan, get the CSV, so you can see what's about to change, then make changes and review and QA updated data in HubSpot.

Today, you get all these skills for free.

column3: When i started
column12:
When i started

I spent quite a few months learning GTM the hard way.

Then I realized that most beginners, like I was when I first started, skipped the basics and jumped straight to tactics and execution.

(guilty as we speak 👼)

↳ We get obsessed with tools, channels, and hacks before understanding the core: messaging clarity, ICP clarity, and how the sales motion works

↳ We build content without knowing what buyers actually need vs. what makes us happy to post

↳ We launch outbound campaigns talking about how good the product is, not what value it gives

And then nothing works. And nobody knows why.

Going back to the fundamentals: data foundation and clarity come before anything else.

Messaging before campaigns. Strategy before execution.

So I put together a playbook that covers exactly that.

2026 GTM Playbook

If you are just starting out, or you have been in the space long enough to know that something feels off. This is for you.

Everything I wish someone had handed me when I started:
↳ ICP definition that actually makes sense
↳ Positioning without the jargon
↳ Outbound that does not feel like spam
↳ Inbound that works while you sleep
↳ A 7-day launch plan to get moving

↳ BuiltWith , Wappalyzer , StackShare, Apollo, ZoomInfo, Cognism , and Clearbit.
↳ SEC filings. 10-K annual reports, 10-Q quarterlies, 8-K material events, DEF 14A executive compensation. Great fit for prospecting into enterprise, more unique.

column3: $1M ARR, you needed these 5 GTM rules to stay alive. At $25M+ ARR, you'll be running 62. Here are all of them, mapped to your stage:
column12:
$1M ARR, you needed these 5 GTM rules to stay alive. At $25M+ ARR, you'll be running 62. Here are all of them, mapped to your stage:

→ $0-1M ARR · Founder-led survival (5 rules)
↳ The founder posts 3x per week on LinkedIn from their personal profile.
↳No SDR hire until the founder has personally closed a couple of deals.

What kills you here: Hiring SDRs before founder-led work.

→ $1-5M ARR · Repeatability (10 rules)
↳ First AE closes most deals. SDR books, the founder still backs up enterprise + complex deals.
↳ ICP scored by multiple signals, not firmographics alone.
↳ First inbound-led motion
↳ More content-oriented initiatives to keep building top of the funnel (e.g., nurture, webinars, IRLs)

What kills you here: hiring SDRs before the founder's playbook is documented. Adding tools instead of fixing the process

→ $5-10M ARR · Scaling (12 rules)
↳ LinkedIn Ads $8-15k/month (≈1-2% of ARR).
↳ Per-account AI research before every cold email, sub-$0.10 per account.
↳ Multi-touch attribution. Last-touch is dead at this scale.
↳ Tech stack: CRM + Clay + Instantly + Common Room + n8n. The 5-tool stack.

What kills you here: treating $5M like $1M. Founder still in deals, no system.

→ $10-25M ARR · Operating leverage (15 rules)
↳ AI in GTM: 50%+ of research, scoring, and drafting is automated.
↳ VP Marketing or Director-level hire. Deep specialization emerges: dedicated demand gen, performance marketing, product marketing, and ABM as separate roles (not generalists).
↳ CAC payback target: under 18 months.

What kills you here: ABM without GTM + RevOps support

→ $25M+ ARR · Optimization (20 rules)
↳ 3+ ICP segments mapped, prioritized by signals per segment.
↳ Dedicated outbound team: 8-15 SDRs, 20-30 AEs (1 SDR : 2-3 AE ratio), regional split.
↳ LinkedIn Ads $50-100k+/month (≈1-2% of ARR). Full-funnel: brand + TL + standard + retargeting + ABM + competitor conquest.
↳ Customer Success owns expansion. Sales owns new logos.

What kills you here: SDR factory without quality control: burning CAC and brand at the same time. Marketing in silos: demand gen, brand, content, and ABM without one owner.

My design team put the rest of the tips in this awesome image.

column3: Most B2B GTM runs channels in isolation. Demand gen, outbound, retention all in their own silos.
column12:
Most B2B GTM runs channels in isolation. Demand gen, outbound, retention all in their own silos.
Here are the 6 stages we connect into one loop, so each one makes the next cheaper.

Here's the breakdown:

→ Stage 1 · Generate demand
↳ Content, paid, outbound, partnerships
↳ Where does your buyer actually spend time? Start there.

→ Stage 2 · Identify who's paying attention
↳ Lead magnets, forms, social engagement, and organic followers
↳ If traffic doesn't turn into something you own, you can't capture it

→ Stage 3 · Stay in the room
↳ SDR sequences, newsletter, community, events, retargeting
↳ Most of the pipeline you need this quarter is already in your database
↳ They're just not ready yet, so nurture + (always) stay top of mind

→ Stage 4 · Earn the trust
↳ Sales-led: get the call booked
↳ Product-led: get them to first value (e.g Amplitude )
↳ Before either happens, they've already read your case studies, watched your demo, and used your free tool

→ Stage 5 · Score and route automatically
↳ Clay and HubSpot score every inbound signal against your ICP
↳ High fit goes to sales immediately
↳ Low fit goes back into nurture
↳ 90% of this doesn't need a human in the loop anymore

→ Stage 6 · Land and expand
↳ Retention is underrated as a growth channel
↳ Clients who stay become your case studies (e.g Swarm, Clustr)
↳ Case studies become your best top-of-funnel

Why the whole thing compounds:

↳ Cold outreach converts better when prospects already recognize the brand
↳ The content that performs organically tells you what to put a budget behind
↳ Your paid audiences and your outbound lists overlap more than most people realize
↳ Every engagement signal from every stage sharpens the next campaign

We use HubSpot and Clay for scoring and routing in stage 5. Common Room, Trigify, and Factors.ai  to pick up 1st-party engagement signals in stage 2.

Each one makes the next stage more efficient.

Not every path needs to be running. But every stage should have at least one.

Which two stages in your loop aren't talking to each other yet?

P.S. Comment "LOOP" and I'll DM the full map + tool stack!

column12:
Anthropic launched Claude Design on April 17th. Since then, every Growth Today LinkedIn post has been generated by it. Our output is up 4x. Now you can have the same:

I worked with both a designer and a dedicated copywriter, and I wasn't happy with the output.

Slow. A lot of back and forth. Not always on brand. Not always in my voice.

And even when the output came back, I'd spend another hour or two reviewing,
making sure it was on-brand and matched the way I'd actually say it.

So I sat down with @Gaze. I told her:

"We know what we sell, who we sell to, and what works on LinkedIn in our space. Can you automate 90% of the research, ideation, and creation?"

I showed her how I'd fine-tuned Claude  Design and walked her through the whole process. 
Then we automated it.

What used to take 4 hours takes 15-30 minutes per finished LinkedIn post: visual + copy. 
Now get 2-4x more output, with better quality.

3 things this changed for us:

↳ We stopped briefing. We generate the full design through Claude (don't even need to touch Figma , unless it's animation or more complex).
↳ We spend less time on back-and-forth revisions
↳ We generate content for the whole team with one ask in 15 minutes: "Can you generate this for @Jani@, @Nikola, @Prosper, @Simone, and @Alejandro in their own style?"

Results:
↳ 3x more impressions in the last 30 days, averaging 10k-47k per post
↳ 20+ posts shipped this quarter through this exact pipeline
↳ ~30 minutes from copy to scheduled PNG (Scripe handles the queue)

Step 6: Push to campaign
↳ Instantly.ai , Smartlead, EmailBison, Heyreach, lemlist

Best of all?

Step 7: Analyse what converts and feed this back to your sequencer
↳ Call transcripts
↳ Best-performing angles
↳ Flow back into the skills automatically, so your system gets smarter and better over time.

(without rebuilding anything from scratch or training anything again and again)

The architecture is simple:

You = Captain. You define the ICP, set the routing rules, and approve the plays.
MCPs = bridge between you and your tools
Claude = command center. Runs the full orchestration.

column3: 6 Claude Skills. 11-tab GTM strategy playbook. Now you can run it on your own ICP for free:
column12:
6 Claude Skills. 11-tab GTM strategy playbook. Now you can run it on your own ICP for free:

Most GTM teams spend 2 weeks building what Claude builds in 90 minutes.

ICP. Battlecard. Signal library. Messaging matrix. All of it.

Here is the 6-skill system. Comment "ATLAS" and run it yourself today.

Skill 1 → Market Research
↳ Claude searches G2, Reddit, Inc., Trustpilot, and the web with Perplexity, Claygent
↳ Maps your full competitive landscape before you brief anyone
↳ Output: market overview + validated competitor list with differentiators and how to put these into practice in your GTM sequence

Skill 2 → ICP Hypothesis
↳ Test your stated ICP against your actual customer data through HubSpot, Salesforce
↳ 80% of B2B teams focus more on who they want, and not enough on who buys from them, and double down on that IMMEDIATELY
↳ Output: real ICP + pain per persona

Skill 3 → Signal Library
↳ 25+ buying signals across 7 categories + signal combo plays
↳ 3+ stacked signals = 35-40% reply rate. Single signal = 6-8%.
↳ Output: signal stack ready to load into Clay

Skill 4 → Battlecard
↳ Competitor displacement + objection handling per persona
↳ Every cell must be sayable in 10 seconds on a live call
↳ Output: AE-ready battlecard your team will actually use (your Growth, RevOps, Sales, and even Marketing team)

Skill 5 → Messaging Matrix
↳ 18 cold outreach variants from one ICP input (differentiated messaging by industry by persona, by company size, or what matters in your business)
↳ Your solution framing never changes. Your copy changes by persona, industry, and size.
↳ Output: 18 variants ready to use in any 1:1 or 1:many outreach

Skill 6 → Assembly
↳ Packages everything into a formatted .xlsx workbook automatically
↳ Output: [YourCompany]_GTM_Playbook_v1.xlsx

column3: We analyzed 85+M cold emails: Gong, Instantly.ai, Salesloft, Smartlead, Lavender, and our database. The top 10% of cold email senders book 8.1× more meetings than everyone else. Here’s how they do it:
column12:
We analyzed 85+M cold emails: Gong, Instantly.ai, Salesloft, Smartlead, Lavender, and our database. The top 10% of cold email senders book 8.1× more meetings than everyone else. Here’s how they do it:

Subject lines

↳ Keep it under 5 words → 2–4 words gets 46% open rate, 10+ words gets 34%
↳ Write it all lowercase → 11% more opens
↳ Personalize the subject line → 133% more replies than a generic one
↳ Remove: "AI", nuzzwords, numbers, and questions → -9%, -4%, -7% fewer open

Email length

↳ 1 sentence emails: 1.9% reply. 3 sentences: 2.9%. Write 3 sentences.
↳ Keep it under 100 words. It gets ~50% higher reply rate than anything longer
↳ Top reps use "you / your / your team" 29% more than average reps

Personalization type

↳ Activity-based (what they actually did): 24% direct reply rate
↳ Company-level: 9%
↳ No personalization: 2%
↳ "I noticed you are in SaaS" is not personalization

Words that hurt your reply rates

↳ "AI" in the body: −36%
↳ "Platform" or "solution": −26%
↳ Buzzwords: −57%
↳ Write the way you talk on a call (not the way you write a landing page)

CTA

↳ Asking for a meeting in Email 1: −44% reply rate
↳ Making an offer: +28%
↳ One CTA only. Don’t use multiple CTA-s.
↳ The top CTA globally in 2025 was a soft ask, not "book a 30-minute call"

Sequences

↳ 58% of all replies come from Email 1
↳ First bump adds +49%
↳ Email 5 drops −55% vs baseline
↳ 1-sentence bump = 2× more replies than a longer bump
↳ Breakup email: +89% vs a standard bump
↳ Stop at 4–6 emails. End with a breakup, not another pitch.

Comment "BENCHMARK" and I will send you the full data report.

Repost if you know somebody who would benefit from this.

5 sources. 85M+ emails analyzed. Every stat is cited with the dataset size.

Over to you: which of these is the one you are getting wrong right now?

P.S. We build and run cold email systems for 110+ B2B GTM teams at Growth Today. DM me if you want to see how we set this up for your campaigns.

column3: Teams that combine multiple signals close 3-4x more pipeline than teams running funding alerts. Here's the difference, and how to run ABM:
column12:
Teams that combine multiple signals close 3-4x more pipeline than teams running funding alerts. Here's the difference, and how to run ABM:

Triggers are public events.
→ Series A closed. New VP Sales hired. Installed competitor.

Signals are private behaviors from the buying committee.

→ 3 buyers from one account on /pricing this week. Champion engaged 3x with your LinkedIn this week. VP of Marketing downloaded the content.

The problem with triggers: they're public.
Every SDR with a Crunchbase login can pull the same Series B list Monday morning.

By Tuesday afternoon, 40 SDRs sent the same "congrats on the raise" email.

Your prospect ignores all of them.

→ Trigger window dies in 14 days. After that, you're another cold email.

→ Signal window dies in 24-48 hours. If you don't use it in time, they might book a demo with someone else.

Most teams never get past triggers.

Only 25% of B2B teams use real intent data.
The other 75% are still sending "congrats on the raise" emails.

Here's how we run ABM at Growth Today:

1 trigger = the reason to reach out.
3 signals = proof they're considering it right now. (or have a need + urgency)

One Tier 1 account, properly stacked:

→ New VP Sales hired (trigger)
→ 3 buyers from the company on /pricing this week (signal)
→ Champion engaged 3x with your LinkedIn this week (signal)
→ Marketing engaged through retargeting ads (signal)

Four reasons to send an email. Each is strong on its own.

Reply rates: 5-12% on a stacked signal account. 1-3% on a trigger-only account.

The difference: your messaging reads like you've been paying attention. Not like you ran a Crunchbase  export.

Here's where most ABM programs break:

Sales chases the 5 trigger accounts. Marketing has no idea that those are the priority.
The other 95 accounts on the Tier 1 list get nothing.

→ No content
→ No ads
→ No retargeting

When a buyer from one of those 95 finally gives you intent data, nobody sees it.

The fix:

Sales and Marketing run the same Tier 1 list together.

→ Marketing covers all 95 with content, ads, and retargeting. ZenABM , HubSpot
→ Sales puts higher effort on the trigger-stacked accounts. Clearbit Apify 6sense , CommonRoom etc.

That's signal-based ABM.
Not 5 news alerts on the same account in a CRM.

Comment "INTENT" and I'll send you 63+ curated triggers and signals you can slice and dice for your stack.

P.S. Book a time if you run ABM, but it's not connected, and you want to stop wasting money.

column3: March: 150k impressions
column12:
March: 150k impressions
April: 250k impressions
May: 300k impressions

Across the Growth Today team, we crossed 1.2M+ impressions in the last 90 days.

Same people.
Almost the same tools.
The difference was in the structure.

Here is the 4-part framework behind it:

1. One idea. Full stop.

↳ Not five insights crammed into one graphic
↳ Not a paragraph disguised as a bullet
↳ One claim, one proof, one visual

If the reader can't understand it in 3 seconds, they scroll.

2. Structure before design

↳ The headline does the heavy lifting
↳ Every line supports the headline
↳ The takeaway is visible instantly

Design should amplify the message.

Not replace it.

3. Scannable at a glance

↳ Headline sits in the top third
↳ Max ~30 words of body text
↳ Eye flows vertically, not across
↳ White space is structure, not decoration

If someone needs to *read carefully*, you've already lost them.

4. A reason to save

↳ A checklist
↳ A framework
↳ A repeatable system

Not inspiration.

An asset.

Something they’ll want to reference again in 3 weeks.

Good design gets attention.
Good structure gets results.

Most GTM content has the first.

Almost none have both.

Our internal engine runs on tools like:
Claude 
Apify 
Fellow - AI Meeting Assistant 
Notion 
Figma (for animations)

But tools are not the differentiator.
Structure is.

Most teams are just posting.
The best teams are building content systems that warm the pipeline before outbound even starts.

Comment "ENGINE" and I’ll send the full breakdown.

P.S. If you're posting consistently but LinkedIn isn't driving pipeline, you're likely missing the structure layer. DM me. We build and run LinkedIn content engines for B2B founders and high-growth GTM teams at Growth Today.

column3: Our customers generate more pipeline from warm signals than from cold lists.
column12:
Our customers generate more pipeline from warm signals than from cold lists. 
Here's the 5-step framework we run for all of them:

Most prospects don’t book immediately.
They read, listen, and engage first.

We see it every week with new clients: the warm signals are there.

The way to achieve the same is simple.
Here's the 5-step framework:

1. Capture across channels (not just LinkedIn)
↳ LinkedIn: profile views, comments, likes, connection requests
↳ Website: anonymous visitors  Snitcher  , pricing page hits, competitor comparison visits
↳ Content: who's reading your stuff (Trigify / Expandi / Vector 👻  / CommonRoom)
↳ Account-level: job changes in the buying committee (Clay / Champify )
↳ Email: opens, clicks, repeat reads in your sequencer

2. Stack the signals (if you have a very strong inbound motion, otherwise skip)
↳ 1 signal = reason to reach out
↳ 3+ signals from the same account = prioritize
↳ Multiple people from the same company = absolutely prioritize
↳ Track signal velocity (how fast multiple signals appear on one account)

3. Qualify against ICP
↳ Role + seniority match
↳ Company fit (size, industry, stage)
↳ Buying authority + buying committee structure

4. Reference the insight (not the signal)
↳ Reference the specific insight (if it makes sense)
↳ Don't pretend you don't know they engaged
↳ Keep it short, contextual, and low-pressure
↳ Multi-thread the account (if the top account + multiple engagements have happened)

5. Track the right metrics
↳ Chat-to-response and response to meeting conversion rate
↳ Signal velocity (signal → meeting time)
↳ Signal source mix (LinkedIn vs website vs content vs email)
↳ Account-level pipeline coverage from signals

♻️ Repost so more B2B teams stop ignoring warm signals.

P.S. We build and run these outbound + content systems for $5M+ ARR B2B GTM teams.

column3: LinkedIn has 1.3B users.
column12:
LinkedIn has 1.3B users.

Only ~1% post weekly.

Even fewer turn it into a pipeline.

In the last 90 days, our team generated 1.2M+ impressions from LinkedIn.

Not from posting more.
From running a system.

Here’s the 8‑step LinkedIn engine B2B teams use to break into the top 1%:

Step 1: Pick one market

↳ One ICP
↳ One problem
↳ One GTM angle

The fastest way to fail on LinkedIn is to try to speak to everyone.

Step 2: Turn your profile into a landing page

↳ Headline = who you help + outcome
↳ Banner = one clear proof or system
↳ Featured = lead magnet or case study

Treat your profile like a billboard, not a résumé.

Tools: Canva, Figma

Step 3: Lock your content pillars

↳ 3–4 topics maximum
↳ Directly tied to your ICP’s problems
↳ Each pillar connects to your offer

Example pillars we use at Growth Today:

↳ AI‑native GTM systems
↳ Signal‑based outbound
↳ GTM engineering workflows

Step 4: Build hooks that stop the scroll

The hook drives 80% of performance.

Formats that consistently work:

↳ “Most B2B teams get this wrong.”
↳ “We tested this across X teams.”
↳ “Here’s the system behind X result.”

Tools: Scripe, Claude Code

Step 5: Turn posts into assets

The best posts are reference frameworks.

↳ checklists
↳ diagrams
↳ systems
↳ playbooks

If someone can’t explain why they saved it, it won’t get saved.

Step 6: Design for scanning

↳ headline visible at 40% zoom
↳ <30 words on the graphic
↳ eye flows top → bottom

Structure drives performance more than design.

Tools: Figma, Canva 

Step 7: Repurpose the winners

One strong post becomes:

↳ infographic
↳ carousel
↳ thread
↳ newsletter
↳ video

We refresh top posts every 60–90 days.

Tools: Claude

Step 8: Systemize the engine

Our stack runs weekly:
↳ Apify scraping trending posts
↳ Claude drafting frameworks
↳ Scripe scheduling publishing
↳ Figma generating visuals

So when we post, it’s not random.
It’s a predictable distribution.

LinkedIn growth is not about posting more.
It’s about building a content engine.

column3: Claude Code vs Clay vs n8n vs HubSpot
column12:
Claude Code vs Clay vs n8n vs HubSpot
Most serious GTM teams will want to graduate from a single tool to a comprehensive system. Here's the breakdown:

Step 1: Claude Code
↳ We use it to build the logic layer: skills, automations, scoring rules, CRM hygiene workflows
↳ Best for: building GTM systems, outbound agents, ABM routing, repeatable ops work
↳ If we need 32 HubSpot hygiene skills or 24 ABM agents, this is where we build them

Step 2: Clay
↳ We use it to source TAM, enrich accounts, stack signals, and score ICP fit
↳ Best for: list building, enrichment, signal capture, account research
↳ If we want one table with hiring, funding, website visits, and buying committee data, Clay does that job

Step 3: n8n
↳ We use it to move data between tools and trigger actions in real time
↳ Best for: workflow automation, routing, alerts, and handoffs between sales and marketing
↳ If a pricing page visit should create a task in HubSpot and alert Slack, n8n runs the flow

Step 4: HubSpot
↳ We use it as the system of record for contacts, stages, ownership, and pipeline
↳ Best for: CRM, reporting, lifecycle tracking, team execution
↳ If the data never lands in HubSpot cleanly, the rest of the stack breaks fast

For most B2B teams we see, one operator or a small RevOps / GTM team builds the system.

Some experts to follow:
@Jan Oberha

That team usually builds the workflows in Claude Code, structures the data in Clay, and connects everything.

P.S: We build and fine-tune these engines for $15M B2B teams, and we work closely with your Sales / RevOps or BizDev leader. DM me if you need more 1) bandwidth or 2) want to fine-tune and operationalize what you’ve got.
